refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication reliability within the customer-defined packet loss acceptance criterion.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation capability and (2) meeting a specific quality metric (packet loss limit). The refined FR isolates the functional requirement—maintaining communication reliability during teleoperation—as a single, atomic obligation in the problem domain. The acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1% or customer-defined target) is a measurable constraint on that requirement, not part of the FR itself. This separation allows the FR to remain solution-neutral while the acceptance criterion guides verification.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation sessions under agreed network conditions (latency, bandwidth, interference profiles) and measure end-to-end packet loss across representative operational scenarios. Verify that measured packet loss does not exceed the customer-defined threshold. Document network conditions, test duration, payload types, and loss statistics.
sub_frs: The NGHE solution shall enable real-time command transmission from tele-operator to remote equipment.
The NGHE solution shall enable real-time telemetry and sensor feedback transmission from remote equipment to tele-operator.
The NGHE solution shall detect and report communication link degradation or loss to the tele-operator and safety system.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission based on real-time loss detection and network condition feedback
DP2: Establish a dual-path communication architecture with automatic failover and load balancing across independent network routes
DP3: Deploy forward error correction (FEC) encoding with configurable redundancy levels tuned to measured packet loss rates
DP4: Implement application-level acknowledgment and timeout-driven retransmission with exponential backoff and jitter
DP5: Establish a tiered quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization scheme that isolates critical teleoperation commands from non-critical telemetry
DP6: Combine packet-level redundancy (DP3) with selective retransmission (DP1) and QoS prioritization (DP5) in a layered reliability stack
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP6: Implement a layered reliability architecture comprising (1) forward error correction at the packet encoding layer with loss-adaptive redundancy tuning, (2) application-level selective retransmission with exponential backoff for unrecovered packets, and (3) QoS-based command prioritization that ensures critical teleoperation directives meet the acceptance criterion independently of non-critical telemetry flows

potential_sources: Coupling between FEC redundancy level and retransmission timeout thresholds—mitigated by decoupling tuning parameters into separate control loops (FEC adapts to measured loss; retransmission adapts to latency)
Coupling between QoS prioritization and packet scheduling—mitigated by defining QoS as a logical separation of command vs. telemetry streams with independent reliability targets
Coupling between network condition feedback and redundancy adaptation—acceptable tight coupling; feedback loop is internal to the reliability module and does not propagate to functional domain
coupling_check.notes: DP6 avoids monolithic single-mechanism designs (e.g., DP1 or DP3 alone) that would fail under diverse network conditions. Layering ensures graceful degradation: FEC handles burst loss; retransmission handles isolated packet drops; QoS ensures mission-critical commands are protected even if telemetry is degraded. Decoupling of tuning parameters allows independent optimization without redesign.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network conditions remain stable enough for feedback-based adaptation to converge within acceptable timescales (< 10 RTT cycles). RISK: Highly dynamic networks (e.g., mobile LTE with rapid handovers) may cause oscillation in redundancy levels. MITIGATION: Define adaptation rate limits and hysteresis thresholds.

ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined packet loss acceptance criterion (≤1%) is achievable given the agreed network infrastructure. RISK: If baseline loss exceeds 1%, no DP can meet the requirement without network upgrade. MITIGATION: Validate baseline loss during commissioning; document network SLA dependencies.

ASSUMPTION: Teleoperation command and telemetry streams can be logically separated and prioritized independently. RISK: If command and telemetry are interleaved in a single stream, QoS separation becomes ineffective. MITIGATION: Enforce protocol-level separation at design time.

ASSUMPTION: Latency overhead from FEC encoding and retransmission does not violate teleoperation responsiveness requirements. RISK: Excessive redundancy or retransmission delays may degrade operator experience or safety. MITIGATION: Define latency budget; measure end-to-end latency under worst-case redundancy.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite network conditions are known and stable during operation. RISK: Unexpected interference or congestion may violate assumptions. MITIGATION: Implement runtime monitoring and alerting; define fallback operational modes (e.g., reduced autonomy).
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication reliability such that critical command packets achieve ≤1% packet loss (or customer-defined target) under agreed network conditions, while non-critical telemetry may tolerate higher loss rates. The solution shall adapt redundancy and retransmission strategies in real-time based on measured network conditions and shall not introduce latency overhead exceeding [X ms] for command delivery. Worksite safety policies shall define fallback operational modes if reliability targets cannot be sustained.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all teleoperation command processing, reliability mechanisms, and QoS-based prioritization for critical directives; owns the layered reliability architecture spanning packet encoding, application-level retransmission, and command prioritization logic

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives high-level teleoperation commands and directives from operator; Teleoperation module applies QoS prioritization and reliability mechanisms to ensure critical tasking directives meet acceptance criteria

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface for packet transmission/reception; Teleoperation module implements forward error correction at encoding layer and manages loss-adaptive redundancy tuning; receives acknowledgments for retransmission logic

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module reports reliability metrics, retransmission statistics, and QoS enforcement status to telemetry stream; non-critical telemetry flows are deprioritized relative to critical command acceptance

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module escalates critical command acceptance failures or reliability threshold violations to Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment: Teleoperation is the natural owner of command reliability, QoS prioritization, and selective retransmission logic. Forward error correction (FEC) at packet encoding layer is tightly coupled to command transmission and fits within teleoperation's domain. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert escalation and Telemetry reporting leak out; both are appropriate external dependencies. The layered architecture (encoding → application → QoS) is entirely internal to command processing.

Poor containment: Vehicle module typically owns vehicle state, actuation, and sensor fusion—not command reliability or QoS prioritization. Placing DP6 here would require Vehicle to own teleoperation protocol logic, which is a cross-cutting concern. High diffusion: FEC, retransmission, and QoS logic would need to diffuse into Teleoperation for command interpretation, and into Network Link for transmission. Violates separation of concerns.

Poor containment: Safety Services owns safety-critical validation and hazard mitigation, not command reliability mechanisms. While QoS-based prioritization has safety implications, the implementation of FEC, retransmission, and adaptive redundancy tuning is not a safety service—it is a communication reliability service. High diffusion: Core reliability logic would diffuse into Teleoperation and Network Link. Safety Services should consume reliability status via SafetyAlert, not own the mechanism.

Very poor containment: NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not an implementation module. Placing DP6 here would be overly broad and would require decomposition into sub-modules anyway. Massive diffusion: all three layers (FEC, retransmission, QoS) would need to be distributed across Teleoperation, Vehicle, and Network Link. No architectural clarity or ownership.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural and most cohesive owner of DP6 because: (1) it is responsible for interpreting and processing operator commands, (2) command reliability and QoS prioritization are intrinsic to teleoperation protocol semantics, (3) the layered architecture (FEC → retransmission → QoS) operates entirely within the command processing pipeline, and (4) external dependencies (Network Link for transmission, SafetyAlert for escalation, Telemetry for reporting) are minimal and well-defined. The module boundary cleanly separates command reliability concerns from vehicle actuation (Vehicle), safety validation (Safety Services), and network transport (Network Link).
containment_risks: Risk: Forward error correction at the packet encoding layer may require coordination with Network Link module if the network stack owns low-level packet framing. Mitigation: Define a clear interface contract between Teleoperation (FEC logic) and Network Link (packet transmission) to avoid duplication or ambiguity.

Risk: Exponential backoff and retransmission timing may interact with network congestion detection or link quality estimation, which could be owned by Vehicle or a separate network management module. Mitigation: Teleoperation should consume link quality metrics via Network Link interface and adapt redundancy tuning accordingly, rather than owning link monitoring.

Risk: QoS-based command prioritization may require coordination with Safety Services if critical commands have safety implications. Mitigation: Teleoperation owns QoS logic; Safety Services consumes command acceptance status via SafetyAlert and applies additional safety validation as needed.

Risk: If multiple teleoperation channels or protocols are supported, the layered reliability architecture may need to be abstracted into a reusable component. Mitigation: Design Teleoperation module with internal abstraction layers to support protocol variants without diffusing logic across modules.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR specifies a measurable acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1% or customer-defined target) under agreed network conditions. The Teleoperation IM boundary owns all three layers of DP6 (FEC, retransmission, QoS prioritization), enabling end-to-end verification of packet loss metrics against the criterion. Telemetry interface provides observability of reliability metrics and retransmission statistics. SafetyAlert interface escalates threshold violations, enabling closed-loop validation. Verification can be conducted via network simulation, field testing under controlled packet loss injection, and operational telemetry analysis.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. The Teleoperation module is unambiguously allocated ownership of the layered reliability architecture. The containment score (0.92) and minimal diffusion (0.08) confirm that DP6 logic is cohesively packaged within a single module boundary. External interfaces (Tasking, Network Link, Telemetry, SafetyAlert) are clearly defined with directional flow and responsibility delineation. No ambiguity exists regarding which module owns FEC, retransmission, or QoS logic.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Forward error correction at packet encoding layer may require tight coupling with Network Link module if the network stack owns low-level packet framing or serialization. Current design assumes Teleoperation owns FEC logic; if Network Link also owns packet encoding, duplication or interface ambiguity could arise. Mitigation: Define explicit contract specifying that Teleoperation owns FEC algorithm and loss-adaptive redundancy tuning; Network Link owns physical packet transmission and framing. FEC output is passed to Network Link as an opaque encoded payload.

Risk: Exponential backoff and retransmission timing may interact with network congestion detection or link quality estimation. If Vehicle or a separate Network Management module owns link monitoring, Teleoperation's adaptive redundancy tuning could conflict with or duplicate congestion control logic. Mitigation: Teleoperation consumes link quality metrics (e.g., estimated packet loss rate, latency) via Network Link interface as read-only inputs. Teleoperation adapts FEC redundancy and retransmission strategy based on these metrics without owning the monitoring logic itself.

Risk: QoS-based command prioritization logic may diffuse into Safety Services if critical commands are conflated with safety-critical commands. Safety Services may attempt to own command prioritization as a safety mechanism. Mitigation: Clearly separate concerns: Teleoperation owns QoS prioritization for reliability (ensuring critical teleoperation directives meet packet loss criterion); Safety Services owns safety validation and hazard mitigation (independent of QoS). SafetyAlert interface allows Safety Services to consume command acceptance status and apply additional safety checks if needed.

Risk: If multiple teleoperation protocols or channels are supported (e.g., 4G, 5G, satellite), the layered reliability architecture may need to be instantiated multiple times or abstracted into a reusable component. Risk of diffusion: protocol-specific FEC or retransmission logic could leak into Vehicle or Network Link modules. Mitigation: Design Teleoperation module with internal abstraction layers (e.g., protocol adapter pattern) to support variants without diffusing core reliability logic. All protocol-specific implementations remain within Teleoperation boundary.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment within Teleoperation module. The three-layer architecture (FEC → retransmission → QoS) is entirely internal to command processing and does not require ownership diffusion across Vehicle, Safety Services, or NGHE Solution containers. The module boundary is clean and cohesive. External dependencies are minimal and well-defined: Network Link provides packet transmission and link quality metrics; Tasking provides operator commands; Telemetry and SafetyAlert are appropriate outbound reporting channels. No cross-cutting concerns or scattered responsibilities detected. The 0.92 containment score is justified.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP6 directly and completely addresses FR. The FR requires maintaining teleoperation communication reliability within a customer-defined packet loss acceptance criterion (≤ 1%). DP6 specifies a three-layer reliability architecture (FEC with loss-adaptive tuning, application-level retransmission with exponential backoff, QoS-based command prioritization) that is logically sound and sufficient to meet the criterion. The DP is technology-agnostic and does not over-specify implementation details; it defines the solution concept at the appropriate level of abstraction. The mapping is non-redundant: each layer addresses a distinct aspect of reliability (encoding robustness, recovery, and prioritization). Traceability is clear: FEC and retransmission directly reduce effective packet loss; QoS prioritization ensures critical directives meet the criterion independently of non-critical flows. No gaps or conflicts detected between FR and DP.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Formalize the interface contract between Teleoperation and Network Link. Specify: (a) FEC output format and encoding parameters passed from Teleoperation to Network Link; (b) link quality metrics (estimated packet loss rate, latency, congestion indicators) passed from Network Link to Teleoperation; (c) acknowledgment/NACK signals for retransmission logic. This prevents ambiguity regarding packet encoding ownership.

Revision 2: Define the QoS prioritization policy explicitly. Specify: (a) which command types are classified as critical vs. non-critical; (b) priority queue depth and scheduling discipline (e.g., strict priority, weighted fair queuing); (c) timeout and retry limits for critical commands; (d) fallback behavior if critical command acceptance fails repeatedly. This ensures QoS logic is implementable and verifiable.

Revision 3: Clarify the loss-adaptive redundancy tuning algorithm. Specify: (a) how FEC redundancy level is adjusted based on observed or estimated packet loss rate; (b) adaptation timescale (e.g., per-packet, per-burst, per-session); (c) bounds on redundancy overhead to prevent network saturation; (d) interaction with exponential backoff (e.g., does redundancy increase trigger backoff reset?). This prevents ambiguity in DP implementation.

Revision 4: Define escalation criteria for SafetyAlert. Specify: (a) which reliability threshold violations trigger escalation (e.g., critical command acceptance failure rate > X%); (b) escalation message content (e.g., command ID, failure reason, recommended action); (c) Safety Services' expected response (e.g., operator notification, command abort, failsafe activation). This ensures SafetyAlert interface is actionable.

Revision 5: Add a verification plan section to the IM boundary documentation. Specify: (a) how packet loss criterion (≤ 1%) will be measured and validated (e.g., network simulation, field testing, operational telemetry); (b) test scenarios covering nominal conditions, congestion, and adversarial packet loss; (c) acceptance test thresholds for FEC effectiveness, retransmission success rate, and QoS enforcement; (d) operational monitoring and alerting strategy. This ensures FR-IM traceability is verifiable.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, and the FR is sufficiently specific to support verification. However, the gate results identify five critical revisions that must be incorporated before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. These revisions address: (1) interface contract ambiguity between Teleoperation and Network Link (FEC encoding ownership, link quality metrics, acknowledgment signals); (2) underspecified QoS prioritization policy (command classification, queue discipline, timeout/retry limits); (3) loss-adaptive redundancy tuning algorithm details (adaptation triggers, timescale, overhead bounds); (4) SafetyAlert escalation criteria (threshold definitions, message content, Safety Services response expectations); and (5) verification plan integration (measurement methodology, test scenarios, acceptance thresholds, operational monitoring). These revisions are essential to prevent downstream diffusion risks, ensure implementability, and maintain traceability for safety-critical teleoperation. Stopping without revision would leave ambiguities that could propagate into sub-FR decomposition and create rework. Proceeding to sub-FRs without revision risks decomposing an incompletely specified parent FR, leading to incoherent sub-FR hierarchies and verification gaps. Revising the current level (DP and IM documentation) to incorporate the five recommendations will solidify the design intent and enable clean sub-FR derivation in the next iteration.

revise.why: DP6 is logically sound and addresses FR correctly, but its realization (IM) requires explicit formalization of five critical aspects: (1) Teleoperation-Network Link interface contract (FEC output format, link quality metric consumption, acknowledgment/NACK protocol); (2) QoS prioritization policy (command classification scheme, queue discipline, timeout/retry bounds, fallback behavior); (3) loss-adaptive redundancy tuning algorithm (adaptation triggers based on packet loss rate, timescale, overhead constraints, interaction with exponential backoff); (4) SafetyAlert escalation criteria (threshold definitions, message format, Safety Services response expectations); (5) verification plan (measurement methodology, test scenarios, acceptance thresholds, operational monitoring strategy). These revisions must be captured in the IM boundary documentation and interface specifications to eliminate ambiguity and prevent diffusion risks identified in the DP-IM gate. Once revisions are incorporated, the FR-DP-IM chain will be sufficiently detailed to support coherent sub-FR decomposition.

assumptions: Assumption 1: Network Link module owns physical packet transmission, framing, and link quality estimation (packet loss rate, latency, congestion indicators). Teleoperation module consumes these metrics as read-only inputs and does not own link monitoring logic.

Assumption 2: Teleoperation module owns the complete three-layer reliability architecture (FEC, retransmission, QoS prioritization) and is responsible for end-to-end command delivery reliability within the packet loss criterion (≤ 1% or customer-defined target).

Assumption 3: Safety Services module owns safety validation and hazard mitigation logic independent of QoS prioritization. SafetyAlert interface allows Safety Services to consume command acceptance status and apply additional safety checks without owning QoS logic.

Assumption 4: FEC algorithm and loss-adaptive redundancy tuning remain entirely within Teleoperation boundary. Network Link receives FEC-encoded payloads as opaque data and does not interpret or modify FEC logic.

Assumption 5: QoS prioritization policy distinguishes between critical teleoperation directives (e.g., emergency stop, mode change) and non-critical flows (e.g., telemetry requests). This classification is defined within Teleoperation and does not conflict with Safety Services' safety-critical command definitions.

Assumption 6: Verification of the packet loss criterion (≤ 1%) can be conducted via network simulation (controlled packet loss injection), field testing under agreed network conditions, and operational telemetry analysis. Telemetry interface provides observability of reliability metrics and retransmission statistics.

Assumption 7: Multiple teleoperation protocols or channels (e.g., 4G, 5G, satellite) can be supported through internal abstraction layers (e.g., protocol adapter pattern) within Teleoperation without diffusing core reliability logic across module boundaries.
open_risks: Risk 1 (Interface Ambiguity): FEC encoding ownership and packet framing responsibility between Teleoperation and Network Link remain underspecified. If Network Link also owns packet encoding, duplication or interface conflicts could arise. Mitigation: Formalize interface contract specifying FEC algorithm ownership, output format, and encoding parameters. Define Network Link responsibility as physical transmission and framing only.

Risk 2 (Congestion Control Interaction): Exponential backoff and retransmission timing may interact with network congestion detection or link quality estimation. If Vehicle or a separate Network Management module owns link monitoring, Teleoperation's adaptive redundancy tuning could conflict with or duplicate congestion control logic. Mitigation: Teleoperation consumes link quality metrics as read-only inputs and adapts FEC/retransmission strategy without owning monitoring logic.

Risk 3 (Safety-QoS Conflation): QoS-based command prioritization logic may diffuse into Safety Services if critical commands are conflated with safety-critical commands. Mitigation: Clearly separate concerns—Teleoperation owns QoS prioritization for reliability; Safety Services owns safety validation. SafetyAlert interface enables Safety Services to apply additional safety checks independently.

Risk 4 (Protocol Variant Diffusion): If multiple teleoperation protocols are supported, protocol-specific FEC or retransmission logic could leak into Vehicle or Network Link modules. Mitigation: Design Teleoperation with internal abstraction layers (protocol adapter pattern) to support variants without diffusing core reliability logic.

Risk 5 (Verification Methodology Gap): Verification plan for the packet loss criterion (≤ 1%) is not yet formalized. Test scenarios, acceptance thresholds, and operational monitoring strategy are undefined. Mitigation: Develop comprehensive verification plan specifying measurement methodology, test scenarios (nominal, congestion, adversarial), acceptance thresholds, and operational monitoring strategy.

Risk 6 (Loss-Adaptive Algorithm Underspecification): Loss-adaptive redundancy tuning algorithm lacks detail on adaptation triggers, timescale, overhead bounds, and interaction with exponential backoff. Implementation ambiguity could lead to suboptimal or conflicting behavior. Mitigation: Formalize algorithm specification including adaptation triggers, timescale (per-packet, per-burst, per-session), overhead constraints, and backoff interaction.

Risk 7 (SafetyAlert Escalation Criteria): Escalation criteria for SafetyAlert are not explicitly defined. Threshold definitions, message content, and Safety Services' expected response are unclear. Mitigation: Define escalation criteria specifying which reliability threshold violations trigger escalation, message format, and Safety Services' response expectations.
evidence_needed: Evidence 1: Formal interface specification document between Teleoperation and Network Link modules, including: (a) FEC output format, encoding parameters, and payload structure; (b) link quality metric definitions (packet loss rate, latency, congestion indicators) and update frequency; (c) acknowledgment/NACK signal protocol for retransmission logic; (d) ownership boundaries and responsibility delineation.

Evidence 2: QoS prioritization policy document specifying: (a) command classification scheme (critical vs. non-critical) with examples; (b) priority queue depth, scheduling discipline (strict priority, weighted fair queuing), and queue management strategy; (c) timeout and retry limits for critical commands; (d) fallback behavior if critical command acceptance fails repeatedly; (e) interaction with exponential backoff.

Evidence 3: Loss-adaptive redundancy tuning algorithm specification including: (a) mathematical formulation or pseudocode for redundancy level adjustment based on observed/estimated packet loss rate; (b) adaptation timescale (per-packet, per-burst, per-session) and trigger conditions; (c) bounds on redundancy overhead to prevent network saturation; (d) interaction with exponential backoff (e.g., does redundancy increase trigger backoff reset?); (e) validation against packet loss criterion under various network conditions.

Evidence 4: SafetyAlert escalation criteria document specifying: (a) reliability threshold definitions (e.g., critical command acceptance failure rate > X%); (b) escalation message content (command ID, failure reason, recommended action); (c) Safety Services' expected response (operator notification, command abort, failsafe activation); (d) escalation frequency and rate-limiting strategy to prevent alert flooding.

Evidence 5: Comprehensive verification plan document including: (a) measurement methodology for packet loss criterion (≤ 1%) validation (network simulation, field testing, operational telemetry); (b) test scenarios covering nominal conditions, congestion, and adversarial packet loss injection; (c) acceptance test thresholds for FEC effectiveness, retransmission success rate, and QoS enforcement; (d) operational monitoring and alerting strategy; (e) traceability matrix linking FR to verification methods and acceptance criteria.

Evidence 6: Protocol abstraction layer design document (if multiple teleoperation protocols are supported) showing: (a) internal abstraction layers (e.g., protocol adapter pattern) that isolate protocol-specific logic from core reliability logic; (b) interface contracts between protocol adapters and core reliability components; (c) evidence that core FEC, retransmission, and QoS logic remains within Teleoperation boundary across all protocol variants.

Evidence 7: Risk mitigation validation evidence demonstrating: (a) no duplication of FEC logic between Teleoperation and Network Link; (b) no conflation of QoS prioritization with Safety Services' safety validation logic; (c) no diffusion of congestion control logic across module boundaries; (d) clear ownership and responsibility delineation for all reliability mechanisms.
